headline_of_the_day: A slow news Sunday, with one real story worth your time
headline_rationale: The one story today that asks readers to actually do something.

key_points.text: A widely used open-source login tool had a flaw that let attackers skip passwords, and a fix is now available.
A central bank signaled it may cut interest rates once before the end of the year.
Norm here. It was a quiet one today, so the brief is short. Nothing wrong, just not much happened.

stories.title: A common login tool had a flaw that let attackers skip passwords
stories.summary: The bug is in software many websites use to sign people in, and a fix is out now.

article.background: Many websites do not build their own login systems. They use shared open-source tools, which are free programs that anyone can inspect and reuse. When one of those tools has a flaw, every site using it can be exposed at once.
article.what_happened: Researchers found a flaw in a popular login tool that let an attacker sign in as someone else without their password. The tool's maintainers released a fix the same day and urged sites to update. There is no sign it was used against real users before the fix.
article.why_it_matters: You do not have to do anything, but this is a good week to turn on two-step login where you can, since it would have blocked this attack. The sites you use should update on their own.

quote.text: The fix is simple to apply, and we would rather sites patch today than wait for the weekend.
quote.attribution: Sam Torres, a maintainer of the login tool

stories.title: Regulators warn about home routers still using default passwords
stories.summary: A safety notice urges people to change the password their internet router shipped with.

briefly.title: A central bank hinted at one rate cut before year end
A carmaker recalled 90,000 cars over a software bug
A telescope shared its first deep-field image of the year
